coredata.dc:title: Multi-functional ratiometric detection based on dual-emitting N-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Haiyang
Sun, Hongcan
Shao, HaiBao
Liu, Fan
Xu, Shuhong
Zheng, Peng
Zheng, Liang
Ying, Zhihua
Zheng, Hui
Jiang, Yuan
Zhang, Yang
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric fluorescence probes based on multi-emission carbon dots improve accuracy and sensitivity on detecting various environment issues. Herein, a novel dual-emitting N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized from citric acid and urea via a solvothermal method in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The blue and orange emissions of N-CDs in water were modulated, and pure white light-emitting with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.33, 0.33) was achieved. The two PL centers behaved differently for Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ ions, with the limit of detection (LOD) of ppm as fluorescence probes. Additionally, N-CDs displayed unique solvatochromism phenomenon. A new green emission appeared in organic solvents and gradually quenched with the increase of solvent polarity. The ratiometric PL displayed an excellent linear response for detecting water, and the LOD was between 0.003 % and 0.3 % in DMF, ethanol, isopropanol and N-methylpyrrolidone. Furthermore, N-CDs exhibited pH-sensitive response in the range of 4.0–7.0 and temperature-dependent response during heating–cooling cycles between 15 and 70 °C. A simple, efficient and reliable multi-functional ratiometric probe for detecting metal ions, water content, pH and temperature simultaneously was realized. However, there is a need for future application research to overcome the limitation imposed by the excitation wavelength of 330 nm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon dots
Solvatochromism
Water detection in organic solvents
Ions sensing
Temperature sensing
pH sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of pH using N-CDs probe
Detection of temperature using N-CDs probe
Detection of metal ions using N-CDs probe
Detection of water content in organic solvents using N-CDs probe
Detection of water content in organic solvents

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (inset) Gaussian particle-size distribution and (b) HR-TEM image of as-prepared N-CDs-2–10.
(a) Raman and (b) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs-2–10.

(a) Absorbance and PLE spectra of the N-CDs-2–10 recorded at 440 nm and 580 nm. (b) PL spectra of N-CDs growth under varied CA-to-urea ratios. (c) CIE diagram for N-CDs-2–10 under CA-to-urea ratios of 2–10 (inset: photograph under 330 nm excitation).

(a) The relative PL ratio of N-CDs-2–10 in the absence and presence of various metal ions at a concentration of 1 mM. PL emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of (b) Fe3+, (c) Cu2+ and (d) Ag+. The inset in each figure is the linear intensity or intensity ratio with respect to their concentrations.

(a) PL spectra of N-CDs-2–1 in 11 kinds of solvents. (b) Green emission position and (c) intensity ratio (Igreen/Iblue) with respect to ET N of N-CDs in various solvents. (d) PL spectra of N-CDs with the increase of the water content in DMF from 0% to 100% (inset:intensity ratio (Iblue/Igreen) with respect to the water content).

(a) and (b) PL spectra of N-CDs-2–10 solution at various pH values, (c)relationship curves between pH value and the orange emission intensity of N-CDs. The inset is a photograph of N-CDs in various pH values under 330 nm excitation.
(a) the related linearity of temperature-dependent emission ratio and (b) changes in the blue emission intensity of N-CDs-2–10 cycled between 15 and 70 °C.

head.ce:title: Multi-functional ratiometric detection based on dual-emitting N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Lab for Nanoelectronics and NanoDevices, Department of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, Zhejiang, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Lab for Nanoelectronics and NanoDevices, Department of Electronics Information, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, Zhejiang, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advance Photonics Center, School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, Jiangsu, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advance Photonics Center, School of Electronic Science and Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, Jiangsu, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronics and Information, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, Jiangsu, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronics and Information, Nantong University, Nantong 226019, Jiangsu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual-emitting N-CDs with multi-application of white light emission, detection of metal ions, solvatochromism, detection of the water content in organic solvents, pH detection, and temperature detection.

Ratiometric fluorescence probes based on multi-emission carbon dots improve accuracy and sensitivity on detecting various environment issues. Herein, a novel dual-emitting N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) was synthesized from citric acid and urea via a solvothermal method in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The blue and orange emissions of N-CDs in water were modulated, and pure white light-emitting with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.33, 0.33) was achieved. The two PL centers behaved differently for Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ ions, with the limit of detection (LOD) of ppm as fluorescence probes. Additionally, N-CDs displayed unique solvatochromism phenomenon. A new green emission appeared in organic solvents and gradually quenched with the increase of solvent polarity. The ratiometric PL displayed an excellent linear response for detecting water, and the LOD was between 0.003 % and 0.3 % in DMF, ethanol, isopropanol and N-methylpyrrolidone. Furthermore, N-CDs exhibited pH-sensitive response in the range of 4.0–7.0 and temperature-dependent response during heating–cooling cycles between 15 and 70 °C. A simple, efficient and reliable multi-functional ratiometric probe for detecting metal ions, water content, pH and temperature simultaneously was realized. However, there is a need for future application research to overcome the limitation imposed by the excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), which exhibit outstanding luminescence performance such as superior fluorescence, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and environmental sensitivity, have been widely utilized in optoelectronic devices, composite materials, biomedicine, environmental sciences, and so on [1–3]. To meet the demands of complex applications, various CDs of different chemical structures have been developed. CDs with photoluminescence (PL) emission from the visible to the near-infrared region, high PL quantum yield (QY), phosphorescence light and multi-emission are the trends for researchers [4–6].

The excellent PL performance of CDs arises from a wide range of PL origins, including carbon-core states, surface/edge states, molecule states and the crosslink enhanced emission effect [7,8]. Diverse PL centers from the conjugated π-domains, various chemical groups and molecules on the surface or interior of the CDs bring in different radiative transition processes, making it possible to perform two or more PL emissions under one-wavelength excitation [8,9]. These PL components display different sensitive behaviors with exposure to external stimuli. For instance, the protonation or deprotonation of the functional groups may lead to the PL quench under acidic and basic atmospheres. Also, when changing the polarity of solvent, the density distribution of surface electron cloud is influenced by the hydrogen bond interaction [10,11]. Mostly PL from the instinct carbon core-based center possesses good photostability and reversibility, whereas the emissions of the surface and molecule state are easily affected. These sensing information extracted from different types of PL centers, such as changes in PL/absorbance intensity, band position, PL lifetimes and excitation wavelength polarization, make it promising for novel detection applications of CDs [12,13].

Recently, the investigations of the PL sensitivity of CDs on temperature [14,15], pH [16,17], solvents [10,18], small molecules [19] and ions [20,21], have been popular research topics due to environmental issues [13,22]. Among these, ratiometric fluorescent probes based on dual or multi-emitting CDs have garnered significant attention for the built-in error correction of fluorescence signals as the measurement interference caused by machines or background noises can be effectively surmounted [23]. Meanwhile, the rich color variations for PL detection are directly visually exploiting the change of different peak intensities. Dong et al. prepared dual-emitting lanthanum-doped CDs and achieved tetracyclines detection combining the pH-dependence of targets [24]. Feng and coworkers selected neutral red and urea as precursors to prepare CDs and achieved dual-emission bands, providing an effective tool for the ratiometric detection of pH and L-lysine [25]. Wang et al. synthesized CDs by a hydrothermal method achieving in-situ detection of Cr(VI) and lysine [26]. Feng et al. found that two independent emission bands of Mn(II)-doped CDs exhibited unique solvatochromism phenomena when dispersed in different solvents, which showed high potential in discrimination of VOCs vapors [27]. Although detection of various environmental factors has been developed in recent years, the ratiometric fluorescent probes of CDs are still limited in the detection of single or double environmental factors. Multi-functional ratiometric sensing by CDs is greatly needed and its related mechanism should be further discussed in detail.

In this paper, novel N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with blue and orange emission bands were synthesized based on solvothermal treatment of citric acid (CA) and urea using N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) as solvent. The two emission bands were precisely regulated via adjusting reactant ratios and the as-prepared N-CDs emitted bright pure white fluorescence under illumination of 330 nm. They also exhibited interesting response to changing environment such as metal ions, organic solvents, pH values and temperature. The N-CDs were also found to be sensitive for Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ ions simultaneously, possessed the detection limits of ppm. Besides, a new green PL emission band was gradually raised with the decrease of the polarity in organic solvents. Benefiting from the special solvatochromism characteristics, the ratiometric detection applications of the trace water content in DMF, ethanol (EtOH), isopropanol (IPA) and N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) were carried out. The sensing mechanism could be owing to the polarity-responsive intraparticle fluorescence resonance energy transfer (IPFRET) process between the core-related blue emission donor and molecular-state green emission acceptor. Moreover, the detection of pH was achieved with fluorescent color of the N-CDs from blue to white to yellow over a large pH range. Linear temperature-dependent behavior was also observed during heating–cooling cycles between 15 and 70 °C. The dual-emission CDs could be used for multifunctional sensing with high accuracy and capability in diverse environments.

N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetonitrile (ACN), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), ethylene glycol (EG), isopropanol(IPA), N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP), and formamide were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), urea, acetic, orthophosphoric, boric acids, sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and other salts were obtained from Gaojing Chemical Co.,Ltd (Hangzhou, China). All the reagents and solvents were of the analytical grade and used without further purification.

N-CDs were prepared by a facile solvothermal method. In brief, 2 g CA and 10 g urea were dissolved in 30 mL DMF and stirred for 20 min. Then the solution was heated at 160 °C for 6 h in a 50 mL stainless steel autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, CDs were obtained. To remove the unreacted precursor and by products, the obtained solution was centrifuged at 9000 r/min for 30 min. Then, the supernatant was purified in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da). The dialysis lasted for 3 days and the deionized water was renewed every 4 h. Similar experimental conditions were employed with 1, 2, 4, 8 g urea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of pH using N-CDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer solutions with pH between 3.0 and 13.0 were prepared by adding different volumes of concentrated NaOH (0.2 M) to a mixture of acetic acid, orthophosphoric acid and boric acid (0.04 M each). Then, 3 uL N-CDs solution was added to 3 mL as-prepared BR buffer solutions, respectively. These mixed solutions were left to stand for 5 min to ensure thorough mixing before proceeding to subsequent testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of temperature using N-CDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, the N-CDs solution was heated from 15 to 70 °C and subsequently cooled down to 15 °C. Throughout the process, PL spectra were measured at selected temperatures after 5 min for thermal equilibration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of metal ions using N-CDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Standard solutions (1 mM) of Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+ and Zn2+ were prepared in BR buffer solution (pH 6.8) and further diluted to obtain various concentrations. 3uL N-CDs were added into 3 mL buffer solution with a given concentration of ions and mixed thoroughly to form a homogeneous solution. After incubating for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents using N-CDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: Mixtures of DMF and water with different water contents (0 to 100.0 vol%) were prepared. Subsequently, the same volume of N-CDs solution was added to aforementioned mixed solutions respectively. The DMF was substituted with EtOH, IPA, and NMP, and the same experiments steps were conducted.

The microstructural morphologies of the samples were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis with a binding energy of 284.8 eV for the C 1 s level as an internal reference was performed with a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha spectrometer employing an Al Ka monochromatized radiation as an X-ray source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained by a Nicolet iS20 FTIR Spectrometer. Raman scattering spectra were collected using a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectroscope with a laser operating at 514.5 nm wavelength and 10 mW power output. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of the resulting solution was recorded with a Shimadzu 3600 UV–vis near-infrared spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were measured using a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectro fluorophotometer, and the lifetime spectrum was obtained in a FM-4P-TCSPC spectrofluorimeter.

In this work, N-CDs (named N-CDs-x-y, where x-y represents the weight ratio of CA and urea) were prepared by solvothermal reaction in DMF with CA and urea as precursors. Morphology and structural characterizations were performed. As shown in TEM images (Fig. 1 a), N-CDs appear as mono-dispersed spherical particles with single-crystalline structures. The mean size of N-CDs-2–10 is about 3.5 nm according to the particle size distribution histogram. From the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image(Fig. 1b), N-CDs exhibit well resolved lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which is close to the (100) facet of graphite [28]. The Raman spectrum in Fig. 2 a demonstrates the intrinsic characteristics of sp2 carbon with disorder. Two prominent peaks centered at 1352 and 1590 cm−1 are corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively. The high intensity ratio of the D and G band (∼1.0) indicates the existence of structural distortion caused by the doping of N atoms into the conjugated carbon backbone [29]. Besides, weak bands at 2688 cm−1 (2D band) and 2920 cm−1 (D + G band) are also observed, which is characteristic of high-nitrogen content N-CDs within a carbon structure [30].

Detailed investigations into the composition and chemical structure of the N-CDs were performed by FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2b, broad bands around 3427 and 3204 cm−1 are ascribed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. In addition, peaks at 2930 and 2864 cm−1 are considered as CH symmetric and asymmetric stretching. Stretching vibrations of CN and CO groups are observed at 1670 cm−1 [31]. The appearance of 1389 cm−1 is related to stretching vibration of CC [32]. The peaks at 1256 and 1096 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibration of CN [33]. The results demonstrate the surface of N-CDs is rich in hydrophilic groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine groups.

More insights into the surface functional groups and element states of N-CDs were obtained from XPS spectra. The XPS spectra (Fig. S1a) in the full range confirm the existence of three peaks centered at 287.5 eV, 399.1 eV and 531.0 eV, ascribed to the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s respectively. The XPS study reports that N-CDs consist of 52.6 % carbon, 25.1 % nitrogen and 22.3 % oxygen (atomic %), indicating a high N/C atomic ratio estimated to be 47.7 at%. The deconvolution of the high-resolution C1s spectra(Fig. S1b) reveals the signals of CC/CC (284.6 eV), CN (286.0 eV), CO (287.6 eV), and OCO (288.7 eV) functional groups [34]. The N1s band (Fig. S1c) represents the presence of pyrrolic N (399.6 eV) along with a little graphitic N (400.7 eV) moieties [35]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. S1d) contains two peaks at 531.2 and 533.1 eV, representing the CO and CO, respectively [28].

The optical properties of the as-prepared N-CDs diluted in water solution were investigated using UV–vis absorption and PL spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, UV–vis absorbance spectra of N-CDs display three bands at 270, 340 and 400–600 nm. The two absorption bands (Band I and II) at 270 nm and 340 nm are attributed to the π–π* transition of CC and n–π* transition of CN/CO, respectively [36]. The third broad absorption band (Band III) is observed in the range of 400–600 nm, composed of a group of low energy absorption bands, which are assigned to the abundant surface groups of N-CDs. As shown in Fig. S2a, steady-state fluorescence spectra demonstrate that N-CDs in water exhibit excitation-dependent PL property with the excitation wavelength from 270 to 450 nm. The emission spectra display dual emissions with a peak near 440 nm and a band in the region of 520–580 nm. Notably, the N-CDs show two well-resolved fluorescent bands centered at 440 and 580 nm with wavelength excitation at 330 nm. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra of the N-CDs recorded at 440 nm and 580 nm showing the for each located at 365 and around 400–550 nm, respectively, which are close to the absorption bands II and III. The characteristics of absorbance, PL and PLE spectra indicate that the dual emission may related to different emission origins, which will be investigated later. In addition, the intensity ratio of the orange to the blue emission peaks (Iorange/Iblue) increases with the decrease of the feed ratio of CA-to-urea under excitation at 330 nm (Fig. 3b). By carefully adjusting the relative Iorange/Iblue ratio via the feed ratio, the Commission Internationale de L’Eclairage (CIE) coordinate (0.33, 0.33) of pure white light can be achieved under the excitation wavelength of 330 nm (Fig. 3c).

To understand the dual-emissive mechanism of N-CDs, the effect of the CA-urea ratio on the properties of N-CDs was further investigated. N-CDs-2–1 exhibit only absorption band II and two components around 450 and 550 nm in band III (Fig. S2b). With the increase of urea, new absorption bands including band I and component at 506 nm in band III gradually appear, and the intensities of them are significantly strengthened. This suggests that the high supply of nitrogen source improves the proportion of chemical components such as C = O and C = N in N-CDs. The N and O atoms ratio obtained from XPS data also demonstrates similar change in N-CDs, with the C/N/O atoms ratios from 1.0/0.2/0.3 for N-CDs-2–1 to 1.0/0.5/0.4 for N-CDs-2–10 (Fig. S3). Additionally, fluorescence lifetime measurements of N-CDs were also performed (Fig. S4). The average lifetimes of N-CDs are calculated to be 10.2 ns for the blue emission and 5.5 ns for the orange emission, respectively (Table S1). It is reasonably deduced that the two well-resolved fluorescence bands are from different electronic transition processes. The longer fluorescence of blue emission is postulated to arise from the direct electron-hole recombination of instinct carbon-core state comprising primarily sp2 carbon network, while the fast lifetime of orange emission can be assigned to the molecular states from abundant surface groups, especially the amine groups. The FT-IR and XPS spectra discussed above also confirm the molecular state do not stem from a single surface functional group but rather delocalized throughout [37,38].

Selectivity of the fluorescent probe in sensing metal ions was investigated though the fluorescent response of N-CDs (Fig. 4 a). Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ ions rapidly quenched fluorescence of the N-CDs solution. As a comparison, other common metal ions such as Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cr3+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+ and Zn2+ barely influenced the PL emissions. As shown in Fig. S5, the emission intensity of both blue and orange bands continuously quenched upon changes of Fe3+ concentrations, with the ratio of the two bands remaining almost constant. In contrast to Fe3+ ions, the orange PL emission decreased much faster than the blue one with the concentration of Ag+ rising. Furthermore, the variations of Cu2+ concentrations only resulted in obviously continuous quenching of the orange emission, while the blue one maintained. The distinguished of fluorescence spectra suggest that the detection of Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ ions is achievable. For Fe3+ ions (Fig. 4b), the quenching efficiency (I0/I) of both emission bands exhibit an excellent linear relationship versus the concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 500 uM. And for Ag+ and Cu2+ ions (Fig. 4c and d), the intensity ratio (Iblue/Iorange) displayed a good linear relationship within the range of 40–100 uM and 10–100 uM, respectively. The detection limits for Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ ions are 5.7, 0.18, 0.46 uM based on signal-to-noise ratio of 3, respectively.

The fluorescence was quenched by the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process from PL centers to the metal ions [39]. The degree of quenching may depend on affinity of different metal ions and surface groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino). As reported, Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+ ions are tended to surface-coordinate with amino groups, the orange emission is quenched by the photo-induced electron transfer (PET) between the amino-induce PL centre and ions. Similarly, the Fe3+ ions can selectively interact with carboxyl groups to form a coordination bond. As a result, the blue emission was quenched by Fe3+ ions. In the contrast, the blue emission of CDs is totally insensitive to Cu2+ ions as the CDs sensor reported for Cu2+ generally contains N-derived groups but rarely carboxyl groups [40]. Besides, the affinity of Ag+ ions and carboxyl groups is between that of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions, and hence partly quenched the blue component. Although more research is still needed to reveal different response behaviors of N-CDs for Fe3+, Cu2+ and Ag+, the results clearly demonstrated that the dual emissive N-CDs can be acted as a multi-probe for simultaneous recognizing multiple metal ions.

The solvatochromism of as-synthesized N-CDs were observed in non-aqueous solvents. Fig. S6 shows the photoluminescence spectra of N-CDs in 11 kinds of solvents under 330 nm ultraviolet radiation. An obvious tri- or quadru-emission phenomenon in most non-aqueous solvents was found in N-CDs-2–10. An extra green emission band located around 530 nm was observed in organic solutions compared to the blue and orange PL emission in water. Additionally, a shoulder emission around 620 nm appeared when the N-CDs were dispersed in DMF, DMSO, and NMP, respectively.

To simplify the multi-emissive phenomenon in solvents and speculate the solvatochromism behavior, N-CDs-2–1 with only blue emission in water were prepared by inhibiting the orange component with reduced nitrogen doping. The UV–vis absorbance (Fig. S7) and PL emission spectra (Fig. 5 a) of the N-CDs dispersed in diverse solvents were recorded. These solutions show a new green emission band along with the blue emission under 330 nm ultraviolet radiation in non-aqueous solvents. The position of the new PL band appears in the range of 525–545 nm, and Fig. 5b depicts the position with respect to the relative polarity parameter ET N of different solvents. It was observed that the green emission peak had a general trend of red shift with the increase of the solvent polarity. What’s more, the intensity ratio (Igreen/Iblue) of N-CDs exhibits an interesting relationship with the relative solvent polarity (Fig. 5c). It can be divided into two groups with the polarity parameter ET N of 0.5 as a dividing line. When ET N is lower than 0.5, the two peaks of N-CDs are of almost the same intensity (Igreen/Iblue≈0.9). In solvents of strong polarity, the green component is much weaker than the blue one in N-CDs. Besides, the ratio Igreen/Iblue tends to decrease linearly as the relative solvent polarity increase. When the solvent polarity increases further, the green emission may be too weak to be detected, as N-CDs in water emit isolated blue emission.

In order to investigate the dual-emissive solvatochromism behavior in organic solvents, N-CDs in DMF (ET N = 0.40), EtOH (ET N = 0.65), and water (ET N = 1.00) were studied in detail. It was observed that the green band was gradually suppressed with the increase of solvent polarity. TEM images were taken to demonstrate the disperse-state in the three solvents, and no aggregation phenomenon was found (Fig. S8), indicating that the aggregation-induced emission in organic solvents can be excluded in this work [32]. Fluorescence lifetime measurement technique was also implemented (Fig. S9). In the case of blue emission, the decay of N-CDs in water probed at 440 nm is well fitted with a one-exponential function, and the decay of N-CDs in EtOH and DMF are analyzed by a two-exponential calculation. The lifetime τ in water and the sub-decay τ1 in the organic solvents are similar in time-scale (Table S1), which is attributed to intrinsic recombination radiation from sp2-network. From the data, the sub-decay τ2 (≈1.6 ns) in blue emission is found only in EtOH and DMF, and the proportion of τ2 is decreasing with the increase of solvent polarity. This decay process may be related to the intraparticle fluorescence resonance energy transfer (IPFRET) decay [27]. In solvents with ET N lower than 0.5, sufficient IPFRET between core-related emission and molecular-state emission results in strong green emission with similar intensity of the blue one. As solvent polarity increasing, the IPFRET process is gradually suppressed, which brings down the green emission. As a result, the ratio Igreen/Iblue is almost proportionally weakened with the increasing solvent polarity. In high polarity solvents such as water, inefficient IPFRET causes the dominative blue emission of the whole fluorescence and negligible green emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: Water is the most common impurity of organic solvents that can seriously damage the industrial production process. Here, a convenient, efficient, and visible ratiometric fluorescence prober has been developed to measure the water content in organic solvents.

The emission of N-CDs presents dual-emissive property in organic solvents initially. When a trace amount of water was added to different organic solvents, the fluorescence intensity ratio (Iblue/Igreen) was significantly reduced. Fig. 5d shows the temporal evolution of PL spectra with the increase of the water content in DMF from 0 % to 100 %. Interestingly, a continuous increase of the PL ratio and a noticeable change in color are detected upon the addition of a trace amount of water. The fluorescence ratio is linear with respect to the water content in the range from 20 % to 100 % (Iblue/Igreen = 0.012[C] + 2.27, R2 = 0.997, where [C] represents the water content (v/v-%)). The LOD is calculated to be 0.004 % based on signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Furthermore, the visual and quantitative detection of water in EtOH, NMP, and IPA based on N-CDs were also carried out with the similar procedure. As shown in Fig. S10, the N-CDs display excellent response to water in the range of 10 %–100 % for EtOH, 10 %–75 % for IPA, and 30 %–100 % for NMP. The LOD values for water in N-CDs are calculated as 0.3 % in ethanol, 0.003 % in IPA, and 0.003 % in NMP, respectively. As shown in Table S2, the fluorescent sensor of this work combines wide linear detection ranges with high sensitivity, showing much better performance than that of most previously reported methods based on CDs. Benefiting from the unique solvatochromism property of N-CDs, the universal applications as visual ratiometric fluorescence prober for detecting the water content in organic solvents can be performed.

The pH-response properties of N-CDs were studied in different acid and alkaline environments under the excitation of 330 nm. It is interesting to note that emissions of N-CDs at 440 and 580 nm are both pH-sensitive. With the pH change from 7.0 to 3.0, the blue emission peak continuously red shifts while the orange one blue shifts (Fig. 6 a). Additionally, the orange emission quenches faster than the blue one. When the pH changes from 7.0 to 13.0, the blue emission peak generates a visible bathochromic shift along with a significant decrease in PL intensity (Fig. 6b). Meanwhile, the peak and intensity of the orange emission remain almost unchanged. This phenomenon may be caused by the protonation and deprotonation of different functional groups on the N-CDs surface [16,41]. Under acidic conditions, the carboxy group is protonated resulting in a red shift of the blue emission peak. Simultaneously, the protonation of the amine groups causes the blue shift of orange emission [42]. Under an alkaline environment, it is expected that the deprotonation of hydroxyl group results in the red shift of the blue emission with increased pH value [43]. The abundant surface groups on N-CDs response differently to environment, providing opportunities for fluorescence detection of pH factors.

The digital photos in Fig. 6c exhibit a gradient color change from light blue to white to yellow along with the pH shifting from 3.0 to 13.0. The according CIE chromaticity diagram based on the emission spectrum is shown in Fig. S10. Furthermore, a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.997) is obtained by linear fitting the orange emission intensity of N-CDs (Iorange) in the range of pH 4.0–7.0. The result indicates that the detection process can be carried out over the acidic pH range with high sensitivity.

The fluorescence property of N-CDs at temperatures between 15 and 70 °C was analyzed. Measurements were conducted with both increasing and decreasing temperatures. As the temperature increase (Fig. S11a), the two emissions decrease gradually, with the orange emission quenching faster than the blue one. As reported in reference, the population of non-radiative channels is enhanced with the increases of temperature, resulting in the thermal quenching of CDs emission [41,44]. During the cooling process (Fig. S11b), both emissions integrally increase.

The blue emission was chosen as the probe for thermometer applications. A great linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity (Iblue/I0) and temperature is determined (Fig. 7 a), where I0 and Iblue are the PL intensities of 440 nm at the starting and elevated temperatures under 330 nm ultraviolet radiation, respectively. The linear regressive equations for the heating and cooling process are both described as: Iblue/I0 = -0.007[T] + 1.1, where [T] represents the temperature (°C). The thermal resolution of the blue emission is calculated to be 0.73 %°C -1 over the entire temperature range. To illustrate the reusability of N-CDs based temperature probe, the luminescent switching operation of N-CDs between 15 and 70 °C was repeated for more than five consecutive cycles. As shown in Fig. 7b, the blue emission recovers to the initial intensity when the temperature falls back to 15 °C in a complete heating–cooling cycle. The blue emission of N-CDs from the carbon-core state was found to be reversible and restorable during consecutive heating–cooling cycles, making it a promising visible temperature probe.

In summary, a kind of nitrogen doped CDs have been fabricated via a one-step hydrothermal procedure. Parallel experiments revealed that the blue and orange emission peaks were derived from the introduction of core-state and molecular-state, respectively. Notably, both emissions simultaneously exhibit ions-, pH- and temperature-responsive PL behavior, but with different variation trends, making the N-CDs a promising nanoprobe for real-time tracking. Additionally, a polarity-responsive IPFRET system was established by core-related blue emission donor and molecular-state green emission acceptor. Benefiting from rich surface groups, diverse fluorescent centers and different interaction to various environment factors, the dual emission of N-CDs makes it suitable for the multi-applications of ratiometric detecting multi-ions, trace water content in organic solvents, pH and temperature.

Haiyang Wang: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hongcan Sun: Formal analysis, Validation. HaiBao Shao: Investigation. Fan Liu: Visualization. Shuhong Xu: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Peng Zheng: Resources. Liang Zheng: Resources. Zhihua Ying: Project administration. Hui Zheng: Funding acquisition. Yuan Jiang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Yang Zhang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51702075).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124149.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
